content.problemStatement:
JJ has an array AAA of size NNN. He can perform the following operations on the array **at most** KKK times:


Set Ai:=Ai+1A_i := A_{i + 1}Ai​:=Ai+1​ where 1≤i≤N−11 \le i \le N - 11≤i≤N−1
Set Ai:=Ai−1A_i := A_{i - 1}Ai​:=Ai−1​ where 2≤i≤N2 \le i \le N2≤i≤N
He wants to maximize the value of the minimum element of the array AAA. Formally, he wants to maximize the value of min⁡1≤i≤NAi\min\limits_{1 \le i \le N} A_i1≤i≤Nmin​Ai​.

Can you help him do so?


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and KKK - the size of the array AAA and the maximum number of operations allowed respectively.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output the maximum value of the minimum element of the array AAA that can be obtained after applying the given operation at most KKK times.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 200000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and K. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the maximum possible value of the minimum element after performing at most K operations.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we need at least N‑K elements already ≥ answer. The answers are respectively the 1st, 5th, 3rd and maximum elements of the sorted arrays, giving 5, 3, 4 and 5.
K=0, no operation allowed, so the minimum cannot be increased. The answer is the original minimum 5.
K >= N‑1, we can turn the whole array into the maximum value 6.

solution.approach: An operation can copy the value of a neighbour to the current position. Starting from any element whose value is already at least X, we can spread this value to adjacent positions one step at a time. Each operation can increase the number of positions having value ≥ X by at most one, therefore to make the whole array have minimum ≥ X we need at least (N - cnt) operations, where cnt is the number of elements that are already ≥ X. The condition K >= N - cnt is equivalent to cnt >= N - K. Hence we only need at least (N‑K) elements in the original array to be ≥ X. The largest X satisfying this is the (N‑K)-th largest element of the original array (or the maximum element if K ≥ N‑1). The algorithm: sort the array, compute required = max(0, N‑K). If required == 0 answer = maximum element, else answer = element at index N‑required in the sorted (ascending) array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case for sorting.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it)); K = int(next(it))
        a = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        a.sort()
        required = N - K
        if required <= 0:
            ans = a[-1]
        else:
            ans = a[N - required]
        out.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
